content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Colleen D. Winchester 
Assistant City Attorney 
City of Cupe11ino 
20410 Town Center Lane, Suite 210 
Cupc11ino. CA 95014-3255 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-189 
Dear Ms. Winchester: 
September 20, 2016 
This letter responds to your requests for advice on behalf of City of Cupertino 
Councilmember Rod Sinks (dated September 6, 2016 and September 13, 2016) regarding the 
councilmember's duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the 
"Act").1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
QUESTIONS 
1. May Councilmember Sinks make, participate in making, and influence decisions on water 
rate increases if the councilmember will be subject to the rate increases? 
2. May Councilmember Sinks make, participate in making, and influence decisions 
regarding the rights and obligations of the city and the San Jose Water Company with respect to 
maintenance of the city water system under a lease agreement? 
CONCLUSIONS 
I and 2. Pursuant to the "public generally" exception, the councilmember may make, 
participate in making, and influence the decisions. 
FACTS 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-I 6-189 
Page No. 2 
The city is served by three water providers serving three regions of the city. Each region is 
of approximately equal size. 
(1) Approximately one-third of the city's residents are served by the California Water 
Service. 
(2) The second third of the population is served by San Jose Water Company (SJW) 
facilities. 
(3) The last third of the city's residents are served by facilities owned by the city, but 
operated by SJW under a lease agreement that is effective until 2022 (hereafter referred to as the 
"lease area"). You continued on September 14, 2016, that the portion of the city that is served by 
SJW in the lease area equates to approximately one-third of the properties in the city. Your 
questions are limited to the lease area of the city. The other water areas are not at issue in this 
request. Thus, all future references to water rates and the water system refer solely to the lease area. 
The city council will be considering the following: 
1. SJW's request for a proposed rate increase for the customers of the lease area. The City 
may accept or reject the proposal. 
2. Decisions regarding the rights and obligations of the parties with respect to maintenance 
of the leased system. Pursuant to the lease, SJW is responsible for replacement, improvement, 
repair and routine and emergency maintenance of the leased water system. In addition, the city may 
conduct inspections to assess the condition of the system. In the event that the city determines that 
all or part of the system is not being operated or maintained in accordance with customary utility 
standards, the city will provide written notice to SJW, and SJW will correct any deficiencies. 
Councilmember Sinks' property is located in the lease area. You stated that the city is not 
aware of any facts that make the councilmember's property unique or to suggest that the property 
will be affected differently than any other properties in the lease area. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform their duties 
in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own interests or the interests of persons who 
have supported them. (Section 81001(b}.) Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, 
participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental 
decision in which the official has an interest. The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to 
conflicts of interest arising from enumerated interests in Section 87103. The facts you have 
provided indicate the following interests are implicated by the decisions: 
• 
An interest in real property (presumably worth $2,000 or more). (Section 87103(b).} 
• A personal financial effect, that is, an effect on the councilmember's personal expenses. 
income, assets or liabilities, because of possible water rate increases imposed on his 
property. (Section 87103 (first paragraph).) However, Regulation 18702.5( c) provides that if 


File No. A-16-189 
Page No. 3 
a governmental decision affects real property in which the official has a financial interest, 
the "personal financial effect" standard does not apply and materiality is determined under 
Regulation 18702.2. Thus, we do not further consider effects on the councilmember's 
personal finances. 
Fo,-eseeability a11d Materiality 
To give rise to a conflict of interest under the Act, the effects of decision on a public 
official's financial interests must be both foreseeable and material. (Section 87103.) Generally, a 
financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable and material if the interest is explicitly 
involved in the decision. Regulation 18701(a) provides: 
"A financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to be reasonably 
foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a 
governmental decision before the official or the official's agency. A financial 
interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, 
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement 
to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision 
affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 
l 8702.2(a)( I )-(6)." 
Regulation l 8702.2(a} provides: 
"(3) Would impose, repeal, or modify any taxes, fees, or assessments that 
apply to the parcel; 
* * * 
"(6) Involves construction of, or improvements to, streets, water, sewer, 
storm drainage or similar facilities, and the parcel in which the official has an 
interest will receive new or improved services that are distinguishable from 
improvements and services that are provided to or received by other similarly 
situated properties in the official's jurisdiction or where the official will otherwise 
receive a disproportionate benefit or detriment by the decision ... " 
Water Rate Decisions: Under Regulations I 870l(a) and l 8702.2(a)(3), the decision on 
water rates would have a foreseeable and material financial effect on the councilmember's property 
because his property is explicitly involved in the decision. 
Maintenance Decisions: Regulations 1870l(a) and 18702.2(a)(6) above, if it applied, would 
mandate a conclusion that the financial effect of these decisions would be foreseeable and material 
because the councilmember's property would be explicitly involved in the decision. However, 
while the maintenance decisions would involve improvements to water facilities, according to your 
facts, the councilmember's property would not receive new or improved services that are 
distinguishable from improvements and services that are provided to or received by other similarly 
situated properties in the jurisdiction or would receive a disproportionate benefit or detriment by the 


File No. A-16-189 
Page No. 4 
decision. Therefore, with respect to these decisions, the councilmember's interest would not be 
explicitly involved in the decision. 
With respect to decisions not explicitly involved in the decision, Regulation 18701 
Regulation 18701(b) provides that in general, if the councilmember's property is not explicitly 
involved in the decision, the financial effect is foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic 
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. For example, if the decisions concerning the 
condition and maintenance of the water system could affect the rates that the councilmember pays, 
the financial effect would be foreseeable. 
For purposes of materiality, we have already detennined that none of the "explicit" 
provisions of Regulation 18702.2(a)( 1) - (6) apply to these general decisions. Rather, the applicable 
materiality provision would be Regulation 18702.2(a)( 12) which provides that a financial effect is 
material if it: 
"( 12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and 
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision 
was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the 
market value of the official's property." 
Without the specific facts of a specific decisions, we can only analyze this question 
generally. You must evaluate future decisions to detenninc if ( 1) the decision could require rate 
increases on the councilmember's property, and (2) that in light of the rate increases, a reasonably 
prudent person, using due care and consideration would believe that the decision would influence 
the market value of the councilmember's property. 
Public Ge11erally. 
Section 87103 prohibits an official from taking part in a decision only if the effect of the 
decision on the official's interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. 
Commonly referred to as the "public generally" exception, Regulation l 8703(a) sets forth the 
general rule: A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial interest is 
indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if ( l) the official establishes that a 
significant segment of the public is affected and (2) the effect on his or her financial interest is not 
unique compared to the effect on the significant segment. 
( l) For purposes of your question, Regulation l 8703(b) states that a "significant segment" 
of the public is at least 25 percent of: 
• AH real property within the official's jurisdiction; or 
• All commercial real property within the official's jurisdiction; or 
• All residential real property within the official's jurisdiction. 


File No. A-16-189 
Page No. 5 
You stated that one-third of the city is served by SJW pursuant to the lease. This equates to 
approximately one-third of the properties in the jurisdiction or more than 25 percent of the 
properties in the city. This would meet the significant segment test. 
(2) Regulation 18703(c) provides that a unique effect on a public official's financial interest 
occurs when there is a disproportionate effect on: 
• 
The development potential or use of the official's real property. 
• 
The income producing potential of the official's real property. 
• 
An official's real properties resulting from the cumulative effect of the 
official's multiple interests in properties that is substantially greater than the 
effect on a single interest. 
• 
An official's real property resulting from the official's larger real property 
size when a decision affects all interests by the same or similar rate or 
percentage. 
None of these factors apply to the decisions in question. Therefore, the exception would 
apply to both water rate decisions and maintenance decisions so long as the councilmember is not 
uniquely affected.1 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
JWW:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
eneral Counsel 
'\ 
\ 
John W. Wallace 
Assistant General Counsel 
{gal Division 
2 You also referenced the special exception in Regulation 18703(e)(I) that applies where: ''The decision 
establishes or adjusts assessments, taxes, fees, or rates for water, utility, or other broadly provided public services or 
facilities that are applied equally, proportionally, or by the same percentage to the official's interest and other 
businesses, properties, or individuals subject to the assessment, tax, fee, or rate." While this would also apply to your 
first question, it would not apply to the second question. However, since the general exception applies to both questions, 
it is not necessary to reach this special exception.

1. May Councilmember Sinks make, participate in making, and influence decisions on water 
rate increases if the councilmember will be subject to the rate increases? 
2. May Councilmember Sinks make, participate in making, and influence decisions 
regarding the rights and obligations of the city and the San Jose Water Company with respect to 
maintenance of the city water system under a lease agreement?

I and 2. Pursuant to the "public generally" exception, the councilmember may make, 
participate in making, and influence the decisions.

2. May Councilmember Sinks make, participate in making, and influence decisions 
regarding the rights and obligations of the city and the San Jose Water Company with respect to 
maintenance of the city water system under a lease agreement?

CONCLUSION: I and 2. Pursuant to the "public generally" exception, the councilmember may make, 
participate in making, and influence the decisions.
